title: Eight-page mini zine from one sheet
subtitle: The classic single-sheet fold-and-cut method for a pocket-sized zine
excerpt: A single A4 or US letter sheet, folded and cut in a specific sequence, produces an eight-page booklet without any staples or glue. The method takes under two minutes once learned, makes a pocket-sized zine that reads in the correct page order, and produces no waste. This is the standard starting point for anyone beginning in zine-making.
sourceNotes: The single-sheet eight-page fold-and-cut method for zine-making is widely documented in the zine-making community and DIY publishing tradition. No single originator; the method follows from elementary bookbinding principles. The fold sequence described here is the standard version consistent across multiple zine-making guides.

content.text: requires one sheet of paper, one cut, and a specific folding sequence. The result is an eight-page booklet with a front cover, back cover, and six interior pages. Understanding the
layout before writing or drawing on the flat sheet is essential: the pages do not appear in numerical order when the sheet is lying flat.

items.name: One A4 sheet (or US letter), 80 to 100 gsm
Craft knife and cutting mat for the one cut

content.text: Before folding, plan where each page goes on the flat sheet. Fold the sheet in half lengthwise (hot-dog fold), then in half again widthwise (hamburger fold), then once more widthwise. Unfold completely and hold it landscape. The eight panels from left to right, top row: page 4, page 5, page 6 (or back cover), page 1 (or front cover). Bottom row (which reads upside down on the flat sheet): page 3, page 2, page 7, page 8. Numbering varies by convention; the key point is that the front cover panel and back cover panel are at the right and left of the top row when you hold the sheet landscape with the long fold at the bottom.
Fold the sheet in half lengthwise (long edge to long edge). Crease firmly with the bone folder.
Fold the resulting strip in half widthwise (short edge to short edge). Crease.
Unfold the last fold only, so the sheet is back to the half-folded strip.

Cut along the centre fold line, the lengthwise crease, from the folded edge to the centre point of the strip. Cut exactly to the centre, not beyond. This cut runs halfway along the strip. Use the craft knife and ruler against the mat for a clean cut.

Refold the strip at the widthwise crease (the fold made in step 2). Hold the strip at each short end and push the ends toward each other so the cut opens and the sheet buckles into a plus-sign shape. Continue pushing until the four wings fold into a single stack of pages.
Bring the pages together in a single fold at the spine, the
. Crease the spine firmly with the bone folder.

attrs.intro: Problems with the single-sheet eight-page fold.

items.fix: Unfold and identify which face of the sheet was up when you began. Consistent fold direction (always fold toward the same face) is the variable most likely to cause page-order confusion.
items.cause: The sheet was folded upside down or the initial fold direction was wrong.
items.symptom: The pages appear in the wrong order when the zine is assembled

items.fix: Mark the midpoint of the strip with a pencil before cutting. The cut should end exactly at the centre fold intersection. A cut that goes too far creates a book with a gap in the spine; a cut that is too short prevents the sheet from collapsing into pages.
items.cause: The cut did not stop precisely at the midpoint of the folded strip.
items.symptom: The cut extends too far or too short and the booklet will not collapse properly

attrs.heading: Common problems
